sentences.text: Hey, good afternoon, everyone.
Good afternoon, gallery team.
Just gonna wait another minute or so before we get started, but welcome to the apartment IQ training for the Gallery residential team.
Looking forward to walking everybody through the apartment IQ platform.
You should all be seeing the screen for apartment IQ and your gallery residential dashboard, which it looks like a lot of you should already be familiar with based on the login behavior so far.
Really excited to see that folks have uh logged in already.
We'll be getting started in 1 minute as we're looking to see folks last minute getting in here, and then I'll be sure to keep an eye on the attendees just to ensure we get everybody into the room.
We'll start at 50.
All right, let's get started.
Uh, good afternoon again folks.
Uh, my name is Phil Pinto.
I'm the customer success manager that has been working with Blake, Robert, and some folks at the gallery leadership team to roll apartment IQ out to the portfolio for Gallery Residential.
Um, welcome to the training today.
This is the first of several trainings that we'll be offering for your team.

Uh, we have a number of training scheduled through the end of January, as well as a couple of training resources that will be available for you after the fact, uh, and a lovely support team who's able to handle any login issues that you may be having or access issues that you may be having, uh, so lots of resources for you and the team to make sure that you're already on apartment IQ.
We're starting here at the dashboard page for the market survey.
Today's training is going to cover uh everything about the market survey tool.
When you log into apartment IQ, you'll come here to your dashboard where you'll see all the properties that you have access to that have been provisioned to you.
We'll click into one, for example, to just get started opening things up here.
When you open a market survey, you'll see 4 tabs across the top, which are your main navigation pages.
You start on the digitized market survey.
We'll start explaining all about what happens here in the digitized market survey.
Across the first row of options you have your filters for property.
If you want to isolate a specific set of properties, perhaps you want to isolate a specific submarket potentially, you're able to go ahead and make changes there.
We have a filter here for a variety of different unit metrics.
If we're looking to isolate the information on our bed types perhaps by bathrooms, even minimum sizing rent.
Values to A date available even your build and unit style, if we wanted to perhaps isolated renovate units or we wanted to isolate classic units and take out penthouse units, for instance.
Finally, we have the main customized metrics value which you're able to go ahead and I'll do that myself, select all the metrics that apartment IQ has for you in this dashboard.

I would recommend starting with all of them, for instance, and then working backwards to eliminate those that you're not interested perhaps on your particular run through or your particular analysis here.
On the far right hand side, what can we do here?
We have the ability to edit comps.
Apartment IQ has been generated with a list of comps based on we've heard from your leadership teams and what you've submitted, but these are not at all set in stone.
You're always able to come in here and make changes.
When we enter the edit comps menu, you'll see a high level view of where your comp set is an interactive map here where you can identify potential new comps.
Adding a comp is as easy as either hovering over that add comp function.
Or coming to this left hand side.
Here you'll see a menu that describes a couple of key metrics about the properties in your concept.
We start with a comp score, which is an apartment IQ similarity score that allows us to go ahead and create our own value uh for how to maybe measure and benchmark potential properties as comps.
We also have basic information and some basic unit attributes as well, so that you can decide and explore from this page what might be other potential fits for your concept.
Removing a comp that's no longer relevant is as easy as hitting the red X and selecting remove.

You'll also find that we have similar filters here to what you saw in our market survey, including a build style preset and specific amenities you might want to filter for as you're looking for new comps.
Once you're done here, you return to your market survey, where you're able to go ahead and export this information via a PDF report.
I like to consider the PDF report our executive summary of what's happening in this market survey.
We also have for you an Excel report.
The Excel report's really interesting in that it's matching the configuration that you have set here.

So if you have a couple of metrics customized, you have this ordered a certain way, maybe you have some filters enabled, the Excel report will be exported to match exactly how you have your current market survey configured.
To give you an idea of what the PDF looks like, we'll pop that open really quick to see.
You'll find it first thing, prepared for Gallery residential in the top right left hand corner, so it's branded for you.
Stock photo from your property and then the date that this information was collected.
That's particularly helpful to remember because with apartment IQ we're scraping for property websites every single day, every single evening.
So whenever you log into apartment IQ you're always looking at the most up to-date information as of the prior evening.

As you scroll into this PDF executive summary, you'll see your geographic area, 4 key benchmarking KPIs, and then we'll have our trend information alongside the metrics so that if this was the only document you were looking at, you'd always kind of know what this means, what's happening with a specific piece of information.
If we took a look, for instance, at 755 North, I saw their average concession, they just ended a concession.
I can see that at Bass lofts, for instance, uh, the average NER is down 6% from last week, and so on.
We'll go into the bed types as well.

And then finally there will be a single concessions page where you can see all about the concessions, particularly helpful for folks in marketing, folks in operations we're looking to time our concessions really well.
Since the PDF you can export this, you can print it and email it as an attachment to share with whomever is relevant.
Any questions so far about how we can manipulate the data in our market survey and then export our market survey?
We'll go ahead and take a break and take a look at the chat perhaps.
Definitely feel free to come off mute if you have any questions, we can definitely talk about any of these as we're going through.
Otherwise I'll keep going and we'll talk a little bit more about what you can see in a market survey and what actually is comprising this data.
The first things you'll notice when you're reading your market survey is that we read from left to right.

We have first a comp average column which will average out all the information on a particular metric for each defined category here, so that you can quickly benchmark your property's performance against the group average, as well as the specific values across the group.
You'll notice your property is always highlighted in this purple.

As we change our metrics, and I'll take the first two as an example, by default, your market survey will be sorted by the distance from low to high that these properties are from your subject property.
If we were to change this to your built, for instance, you'll notice how the subject property moves all the way to the right and bears a new badge.
It's ranked 7th in this particular metric.
Perhaps we have a smaller screen.
Perhaps we have way more comps than this.
Maybe we have 1012 comps.
It can be helpful to pin our subject property using the pin icon to the left.
This way we're always scrolling against it, and we're always comparing things to our subject property.
As we continue along, you'll find Google reviews, for instance, which we'll link out right to Google Maps.
If you want to dive into the details here, can be a great way to keep on top of how your existing reviews are going, if you're doing a review management campaign perhaps this month.
Great way to keep an eye on how that's improving.
As we scroll through, a couple of different dollars per square foot values, including rent per square foot and ER per square foot.

Scrolling down, advertise occupancy based on all the units that we can see available listed on a property's website against the total unit values that we know, least percentages and exposures over the next 30 and 60 days.

And then finally we get into pieces like what units are available, which of those units are vacant, for instance, and how many applications have we had versus relisted units and ultimately leases in the last 7 days.
Anywhere you see an underlined value, that's a piece that we can go ahead and engage with.
So if I want to drill down and understand what are the 9 available units that are being picked up here by apartment IQ, I can go ahead and click on that value and see specifically the unit breakdown.
What is included?
We have, you know, 217, you know 219, for instance, I want to know what is the pricing history, what is the pricing behavior here?
Again, everywhere you see that underline, you're able to drill down and see what's going on here.
So this unit, particularly 6 days on market doesn't look like pricing has changed much, but if we wanted to find a unit that maybe uh was doing a little bit more.
We have here and can see that this unit has been on the market for 125 days.
The NER has changed it a little bit.
There have been some concessions that were relevant at one point.
And see what the behavior has been there.
As well as on the floor plan level.
So not just the specific unit, but what's happening in the floor plan.
Where you see breaks in this particular graph is where we did not have availability being picked up.
Anytime you might have a question about what one of these metrics is measuring, you can use your cursor to mouse over the metric, and it will tell you and define for you how we're considering these.
We also have a bunch of helpful definitions in our help center, which you can access at the bottom left hand corner.
Here, I always recommend folks start with our FAQ and glossary of terms so that we can understand a little bit about how we're defining a couple of metrics here.
Finally, our support team is also available for you.
In the bottom right hand corner, the live chat function that you can engage with if you have any questions about a particular piece here in your set of information.

Scrolling through past our property information, we come down to the pricing and availability, where you'll see at the top, another similar set of drop-down filters where we can start displaying this information by lowest rent, by the average or by the absolute minimum.
You'll see how all your values and calculations change depending on how we're showing the data and how we're grouping this information.

Do we want to group everything by bed type, so that I can quickly see within a one bedroom apartment floor plan, for instance, what the values are, or would I rather look at this by the metric, so I quickly need to know what the rents and prices are across the entire portfolio set.
As we scroll through this, you will find the same drill downs available.
And then finally we get into some other attributes.
The fees and deposits, for instance, is a newer value where we're able to see common fees that are listed.
This table is ordered from most commonly listed fees to most infrequently listed.
Again, it's all based on what's publicly available and what we can pull from websites.
So most often we're able to see things like security deposits and application fees, least often we're able to see storage fees and utility fees.
There are times where folks might take a look at this place and say, hey, we definitely have a security deposit.
Um, perhaps our pet fees, it's not $500 it's $250 per pet, and it seems like apartment IQ is maybe summing the two values.
You can use our suggestion update tool in the top right hand corner to say, hey, there's something wrong in my metrics.
I can click a specific metric here or I can select other.
And write my comments here.
What's really nice about this functional tool is it tends a, it takes a ticket directly to our data integrity team.

Our data integrity team is there to make sure that all this information is pulling correctly, and they're the ones who are going to help out if there's anything missing or something not quite right or not as updated as you expect it to be.
Finally, the last area in our market survey is this amenities area where we're able to highlight for you amenities in unit, as well as community level amenities.
This information is all part of our exports too, so definitely leverage these exports as needed.
Any questions about our market survey page before we move on to the floor plans area?
We'll go ahead and take a break and take a look at our chat here.
Excellent.
All right, we'll keep going.
The next area in our digitized market survey tool is our floor plans report.

This page is going to provide an additional level of nuance to an information set where we maybe we're used to just looking at things by the bed type, and we can now see the nuance within that bed type and see all the floor plans offered, their trend behavior, and key information like dollars per square foot availability.
On the top right hand side, you're going to notice last 7 days.
By default, this is our first area where we're leveraging the historic information that apartment IQ has.
Since we've been doing this for quite some time, we have up to 3 years and this coming year, it'll be 4 years of historic pricing and availability information which you can leverage here.
I'll go ahead and select the last 12 months just to show you what that does to our floor plan reports here.
You'll notice the studio floorplane type, for instance, got a lot larger as there's more information to populate.
And the rent trends here also got a little clearer.
We're able to see exactly what's going on with pricing within this set of bed types ordered by the square footage values.
So perhaps you have uh a a particular property that maybe you're struggling with your studios.
You might say, hey, my studios are perhaps too small.
It seems like the market is really favoring a larger studio.
You can see that in this market, for instance, towards the bottom.
Bottom half of this table we're seeing a lot of big rent growth, particularly at the bank, the Bass lofts.
Looks like we are really crushing it 21% increase in the last 12 months on that classroom studio floor plan.
So big congratulations there.

Uh, but you'll see at the smaller end of the scale, it seems like rents are dropping quite a bit, uh, perhaps because the market is showing that they're not as attractive floor plans compared to the other side of the studio floor plan.
And we'll see that across all the bed types available.

What's really cool is we do develop for you in the unit mix function, the ability to see the proportion of floor plans that are made up of the total unit mix here, and this again is based on the historic information that we've seen.

As we've been operating a few years, we've probably seen one unit every at least once over the last 3 years, and we remember exactly what it was offered at and what the details were, so that we can bring you this information.
You'll see here when you click on a particular floor plan, you still have that pricing history.
And then at the property level, if I wanted to click in perhaps at the Bas lofts.
I'd find available units, leased units, and then all units in a reconstructed rent roll here.
Again, based on the fact that we've been tracking these properties for years and are able to see all the units that have been offered to reconstruct that rent roll for you.

When it comes to taking a look at this information and ingesting it, by default it's sorted by the floor plan, but if you only wanted to view this information by bed type, it's also possible to do that as well.
Any questions here about our floor plans report and how you might use it?
Awesome.
We'll pop on to the next area, which is our historical report.

This has been a really fun area where we're able to illustrate a lot of the trends and behaviors that are happening in our market, uh, and show what's giving rise to some of the pricing trends and availability trends.
This can be a really great place to kind of see the story in action.

We start with our minimum rent values, which is going to create this graph of the minimum rents advertised and calculated as a medium across all the minimum rents advertised during this interval of time, which we've set at 12 months.
For timing intervals, you'll always come into the top right hand corner.
I like to recommend we go monthly when we're looking at several years.
You'll see that this graph has every single property in our market survey, and we can interact with this graph to isolate particular properties.

So I might clear everything, select my subject property, and then a key competitor to understand what was the behavior, what is the relationship here, how are we doing against them overall, and where are we trending recently?
Alternatively, I could look at this market wide, where you'll see the light green is that uh range or comp median is the green line and then your property is always that indigo.
So you can see how you're performing against the media.
We can contrast this information with our newly listed units as well, which can be a fun way to see how new availability in our market has influenced the pricing behaviors.
We can sort this by newly listed units or the total availability.
And again here, we're able to isolate against specific properties or see everything in our market at once.
It might be interesting to point out, for instance, uh, looked like pricing was pretty stagnant for a while.
They went up here in September and then came back down.
Uh looks like the back half of September to October, which could be reflective of 755 North having added a bunch of availability at that time, and maybe we needed to compete a little bit better.
That availability caused prices to go down.
One potential way to read into this data.
Moving on, you'll find net leased units, which is going to tell you, of course, how the properties and and units have left the market.

And then we can look at that against our net leased units by month if we want to have an understanding of velocity here and really put together a picture of how this has been moving, how properties have been leasing and drilled down into these, if we'd like, of course, by seeing exactly what is making up this value.

The last graph we have here in our historical page is our least percentages, which is of course the number of leased units relative to the total unit count for your property, but you can go ahead and contrast that with exposure to see the total percentage of units that are available to rent against the total units at your property.
We can see how as you interact with this graph, it highlights and changes that you can really pick up on what the picture is, and again, we can isolate specific properties as well.
Any questions here in our historical page.
Excellent.
All right, thanks, Nikki.
Last thing we'll take a look at today is our units tab.
In this area we're able to see the totality of the units that are being collected by apartment IQ.
Everything that we can read as being available across landlord websites or units that have leased in the last 30 days.
We start this table, we have a couple of key performance indicators across the top, just to give us an idea of what that behaviors have been.
We also have this unit data table here, where you'll see the specific unit, its floor plan, how long it's been on market.
Things like data available and the unit specific amenities that we are picking up here.
Through the middle of the graph we'll see pricing information.
So what is the rent and what are any concessions that are applicable, and then what is the NER based on those concession details?
Finally, we can see how often the rent has changed, what the best lease length offered is.
And finally, if it has leased, we can see the date that this has leased.
If I went ahead and sorted any of these columns, I can start to see a picture of what's been leasing over the last few weeks.
Your subject proper units, of course, in this blue.
And then the rest of the concept.
If I wanted to dive deeper, I can use a particular filter here to see just lease unit behavior.
I can order this perhaps based on what has been sitting the longest in the market.
And then if I wanted to go ahead one step further, I can use this true cops filter.
The true cop is a new idea for apartment IQ.
It's an additional filter that allows us to get more apples to apples comparisons on the unit level rather than the traditional property level.

What this means is that we're looking specifically at units that are within a particular differential of your specific units, pricing and square footage to really highlight the most apples to apples comparison based on your availability for a given day.
Any questions here?
Great, I'll come back to our market survey just to kind of center us again here on the main functionality.

As you folks are working through apartment IQ, if you'd like to set yourself up with a particular set of market survey reports, you can use the scheduled report functionality to schedule these to hit your inbox.
It seems we've already got a few folks who have taken advantage of that.
You can schedule the Excel report or the PDF report to go out on a specific day of the month, uh, or a specific date within the month.
As long as you have your apartment IQ email, you can select an individual, select a market survey.
And schedule it.
You'll find the help center again, bottom left hand corner.
You'll find our chat functionality, bottom right hand corner.
And then you should have received your login information on Thursday evening or Friday of last week.

If that link has expired, you can reach out to our support team, or you can go ahead and use the forgot password functionality here at the sign-in page as well.
So using that forgot password link, we'll go ahead and kind of resend that initial invite, so it's the same workflow.
Any questions here so far before we split for the evening, and I wish you all a happy holiday.
Excellent.
Well, I hope this has been super helpful team.
Uh, my name again Phil Pinto.
I'm gonna be here helping and overseeing the implementation of apartment IQ across the portfolio.
